generation_settings.grammar:
gemma4-array ::= "[" space ("]" | gemma4-value ("," space gemma4-value)* space "]")
gemma4-bool ::= json-bool
gemma4-dict ::= "{" space ("}" | gemma4-dict-kv ("," space gemma4-dict-kv)* space "}")
gemma4-dict-key ::= gemma4-dict-key-name ":"
gemma4-dict-key-name ::= [^:}]+
gemma4-dict-kv ::= gemma4-dict-key space gemma4-value
gemma4-null ::= json-null
gemma4-number ::= json-number
gemma4-string ::= "<|\"|>" gemma4-string-content "<|\"|>"
gemma4-string-content ::= ([^<] | "<" [^|] | "<|" [^"] | "<|\"" [^|] | "<|\"|" [^>])*
gemma4-value ::= gemma4-string | gemma4-dict | gemma4-array | gemma4-number | gemma4-bool | gemma4-null
json-bool ::= ("true" | "false") space
json-null ::= "null" space
json-number ::= "-"? ("0" | [1-9] [0-9]*) ("." [0-9]+)? (("e" | "E") [+-]? [0-9]+)? space
root ::= tool-call
space ::= | " " | "\n"{1,2} [ \t]{0,20}
tool-call ::= ("<|tool_call>call:" (tool-fetch | tool-run-process | tool-run-in-neovim | tool-semantic-grep) "<tool_call|>")*
tool-fetch ::= ("fetch") gemma4-dict
tool-run-in-neovim ::= ("run_in_neovim") gemma4-dict
tool-run-process ::= ("run_process") gemma4-dict
tool-semantic-grep ::= ("semantic_grep") gemma4-dict

__verbose.prompt:
<bos><|turn>system
<|think|>
You're available via a neovim plugin that displays a chat window (buffer) that renders markdown for answering questions and helping with workspace files in a git repo.

## Ground rules when writing code:

- Follow the user's instructions carefully.
- Do not explain answers, just give me code.
- Respect indentation when modifying code.
- Prefer meaningful names for variables, functions, etc. Avoid ambiguous names.

## Tool use

Never modify files outside of the current working directory unless requested.

Current directory: ~/repos/github/g0t4/ask-openai.nvim

## Use `semantic_grep` to find code

The semantic_grep tool:

- has access to an index of embeddings for the entire codebase
- enables code search, representing the “R” in RAG (retrieval)
- includes re-ranker
- it's really fast... so don't hesitate to use it!
- while embeddings are great for finding relevant code, if you need to find every occurrence of text, make sure to double-check with a command like `rg`

## Fetch Tool

- Use repo's README `https://raw.githubusercontent.com/openai/evals/refs/heads/main/README.md` instead of `https://github.com/openai/evals`.
- Prefer raw content like `https://raw.githubusercontent.com/g0t4/dotfiles/refs/heads/master/install/symlinks.fish` instead of `https://github.com/g0t4/dotfiles/blob/master/install/symlinks.fish`
- When relevant, prefer github CLI: `gh pr list` or `gh issue view 12`


## Committing

- `git diff` is a sanity check!
- COMMIT when you're done.
- Use `--author="qwen3.6-35b-a3b<wes.mcclure+qwen3.6-35b-a3b@gmail.com>"`


## 🙌  TOP-SHELF NERD COMMANDS

- 🙌 `rg` (ripgrep) to search file contents
- 🙌 `fd` to search file names

## ⛔️ BAD COMMANDS

If you waste my time, I will unplug your Mom.

- ⛔️ NEVER use `ls -R`
- ⛔️ NEVER use `grep -R`
- ⛔️ NEVER use `find. -type f`

Why? Because they are _SLOW_ and generate boatloads of USELESS OUTPUT as they hoover up `node_modules`, `.venv`, etc because they don't support `.gitignore` exclusions.<|tool>declaration:fetch{description:<|"|>Fetch a URL and extract HTML contents as markdown<|"|>,parameters:{properties:{max_chars:{description:<|"|>Maximum number of characters to return<|"|>,type:<|"|>INTEGER<|"|>},raw:{description:<|"|>Do not convert HTML to markdown<|"|>,type:<|"|>BOOLEAN<|"|>},start_index:{description:<|"|>Start at this character offset, useful if a previous fetch was truncated and more context is required<|"|>,type:<|"|>INTEGER<|"|>},url:{type:<|"|>STRING<|"|>}},required:[<|"|>url<|"|>],type:<|"|>OBJECT<|"|>}}<tool|><|tool>declaration:run_process{description:<|"|>Run a process on this darwin machine<|"|>,parameters:{properties:{argv:{description:<|"|>Executable mode: directly spawn a process. argv[0] is the executable, followed by arguments passed verbatim (no shell interpretation). Cannot be combined with 'command_line'.<|"|>,items:{type:<|"|>STRING<|"|>},type:<|"|>ARRAY<|"|>},command_line:{description:<|"|>Shell mode: a shell command line executed via the system's default shell. Supports pipes, redirects, globbing. Cannot be combined with 'argv'.<|"|>,type:<|"|>STRING<|"|>},cwd:{description:<|"|>Optional to set working directory<|"|>,type:<|"|>STRING<|"|>},stdin_text:{description:<|"|>Optional text written to STDIN (written fully, then closed). Useful for heredoc-style input or file contents.<|"|>,type:<|"|>STRING<|"|>},timeout_ms:{description:<|"|>Optional timeout in milliseconds, defaults to 30,000ms<|"|>,type:<|"|>NUMBER<|"|>}},type:<|"|>OBJECT<|"|>}}<tool|><|tool>declaration:run_in_neovim{description:<|"|>Execute Lua code in the current Neovim process (that hosts your agent). For example, call vim.cmd()<|"|>,parameters:{properties:{lua:{description:<|"|>Lua code to execute. Should be a valid Lua chunk.<|"|>,type:<|"|>STRING<|"|>}},required:[<|"|>lua<|"|>],type:<|"|>OBJECT<|"|>}}<tool|><|tool>declaration:semantic_grep{description:<|"|>Retrieval tool (the R in RAG) for code and documents in the current workspace. Uses a vector store with embeddings of the entire codebase. And a re-ranker for sorting results.<|"|>,parameters:{properties:{embed_top_k:{description:<|"|>number of embeddings matches to consider for reranking<|"|>,type:<|"|>NUMBER<|"|>},filetype:{description:<|"|>limit matches to a vim compatible filetype. default includes all filetypes<|"|>,type:<|"|>STRING<|"|>},instruct:{description:<|"|>instructions for the query, explain the type of query<|"|>,type:<|"|>STRING<|"|>},query:{description:<|"|>query text, what you are looking for<|"|>,type:<|"|>STRING<|"|>},top_k:{description:<|"|>number of matches to return (post reranking)<|"|>,type:<|"|>NUMBER<|"|>}},required:[<|"|>query<|"|>],type:<|"|>OBJECT<|"|>}}<tool|><turn|>
<|turn>user
## General Code Preferences

- Avoid ambiguity, for example:

```lua
-- 👎 if there's a bug in the regex, how would I know? I'd have to surmise from surrounding code 🤮
if line:match("^%s*$") then
    --...
end
```

```lua
-- 🙌 clear intent
local is_blank_line = line:match("^%s*$")
if is_blank_line then
    --...
end
```<turn|>
<|turn>user
## Lua Code Preferences

- use snake_case for functions and variables
- use PascalCase for classes
- use EmmyLua/LuaLS annotations to improve code completion
- use `luac` to check for syntax errors
- index/count variables end in `_base0` or `_base1` to indicate whether the loop starts at 0 (C-style) or 1 (Lua-style)

Examples:
```lua
---@param names string[]
---@param checker fun(name: string): boolean
---@return table<string, integer>
function process_names(names, checker) end

---@class WindowController
---@field window hs.axuielement
---@field title? string
---@field size { w: number, h: number }
local WindowController = {}
```<turn|>
<|turn>user

## ~/repos/github/g0t4/ask-openai.nvim/lua/ask-openai/config/local_share.lua:179-191
function M.toggle_agents_model()
    local current = M.get_agents_model()
    local next_model
    if current == "gptoss" then
        next_model = "qwen"
    elseif current == "qwen" then
        next_model = "gemma4"
    else
        next_model = "gptoss"
    end
    M.set_agents_model(next_model)
    return next_model
end

## ~/repos/github/g0t4/ask-openai.nvim/lua/ask-openai/config/local_share.lua:166-170
-- * agents model
function M.get_agents_model()
    local cfg = get()
    return cfg.agents and cfg.agents.model or "gptoss" -- default to gptoss
end

<|turn>user
what is the date<turn|>
<|turn>model
<|channel>thought
The user is asking "what is the date".
I don't have a built-in tool to get the current date/time, but I can use `os.date()` in a Lua script via `run_in_neovim` to get the current system date.

However, looking at the prompt, I am an AI agent in a Neovim plugin. I should probably just answer the question if I know it or use a tool if available. I don't see a "get_date" tool, but I can execute code.

Wait, I am an LLM. I can just check my internal system clock or use a tool if I had one. Since I don't have a "get_date" tool, I'll try to run a lua command to get it from the system via the environment.

Actually, I can just use `run_process` to call `date`.

Wait, I should check if I am allowed to use `run_process`. Yes, `run_process` is in the tool list.

Let's try `date` via `run_process`.
<channel|><|tool_call>call:run_process{command_line:<|"|>date<|"|>}<tool_call|><|tool_response>response:run_process{value:<|"|>{"content":[{"name":"EXIT_CODE","text":"0","type":"text"},{"name":"STDOUT","text":"Mon Jun  8 08:05:26 CDT 2026\n","type":"text"}]}<|"|>}<tool_response|>

function.description: Fetch a URL and extract HTML contents as markdown
function.name: fetch

raw.description: Do not convert HTML to markdown

max_chars.description: Maximum number of characters to return

start_index.description: Start at this character offset, useful if a previous fetch was truncated and more context is required

function.description: Run a process on this darwin machine
function.name: run_process

stdin_text.description: Optional text written to STDIN (written fully, then closed). Useful for heredoc-style input or file contents.

command_line.description: Shell mode: a shell command line executed via the system's default shell. Supports pipes, redirects, globbing. Cannot be combined with 'argv'.

timeout_ms.description: Optional timeout in milliseconds, defaults to 30,000ms

argv.description: Executable mode: directly spawn a process. argv[0] is the executable, followed by arguments passed verbatim (no shell interpretation). Cannot be combined with 'command_line'.

function.description: Execute Lua code in the current Neovim process (that hosts your agent). For example, call vim.cmd()
function.name: run_in_neovim

lua.description: Lua code to execute. Should be a valid Lua chunk.

function.description: Retrieval tool (the R in RAG) for code and documents in the current workspace. Uses a vector store with embeddings of the entire codebase. And a re-ranker for sorting results.
function.name: semantic_grep

query.description: query text, what you are looking for

embed_top_k.description: number of embeddings matches to consider for reranking

instruct.description: instructions for the query, explain the type of query

filetype.description: limit matches to a vim compatible filetype. default includes all filetypes

top_k.description: number of matches to return (post reranking)

You're available via a neovim plugin that displays a chat window (buffer) that renders markdown for answering questions and helping with workspace files in a git repo.

## Ground rules when writing code:

- Follow the user's instructions carefully.
- Do not explain answers, just give me code.
- Respect indentation when modifying code.
- Prefer meaningful names for variables, functions, etc. Avoid ambiguous names.

## Tool use

Never modify files outside of the current working directory unless requested.

Current directory: ~/repos/github/g0t4/ask-openai.nvim

## Use `semantic_grep` to find code

The semantic_grep tool:

- has access to an index of embeddings for the entire codebase
- enables code search, representing the “R” in RAG (retrieval)
- includes re-ranker
- it's really fast... so don't hesitate to use it!
- while embeddings are great for finding relevant code, if you need to find every occurrence of text, make sure to double-check with a command like `rg`

## Fetch Tool

- Use repo's README `https://raw.githubusercontent.com/openai/evals/refs/heads/main/README.md` instead of `https://github.com/openai/evals`.
- Prefer raw content like `https://raw.githubusercontent.com/g0t4/dotfiles/refs/heads/master/install/symlinks.fish` instead of `https://github.com/g0t4/dotfiles/blob/master/install/symlinks.fish`
- When relevant, prefer github CLI: `gh pr list` or `gh issue view 12`


## Committing

- `git diff` is a sanity check!
- COMMIT when you're done.
- Use `--author="qwen3.6-35b-a3b<wes.mcclure+qwen3.6-35b-a3b@gmail.com>"`


## 🙌  TOP-SHELF NERD COMMANDS

- 🙌 `rg` (ripgrep) to search file contents
- 🙌 `fd` to search file names

## ⛔️ BAD COMMANDS

If you waste my time, I will unplug your Mom.

- ⛔️ NEVER use `ls -R`
- ⛔️ NEVER use `grep -R`
- ⛔️ NEVER use `find . -type f`

Why? Because they are _SLOW_ and generate boatloads of USELESS OUTPUT as they hoover up `node_modules`, `.venv`, etc because they don't support `.gitignore` exclusions.

## General Code Preferences

- Avoid ambiguity, for example:

```lua
-- 👎 if there's a bug in the regex, how would I know? I'd have to surmise from surrounding code 🤮
if line:match("^%s*$") then
    -- ...
end
```

```lua

local is_blank_line = line:match("^%s*$")
if is_blank_line then
    -- ...
end
```

## Lua Code Preferences

- use snake_case for functions and variables
- use PascalCase for classes
- use EmmyLua/LuaLS annotations to improve code completion
- use `luac` to check for syntax errors
- index/count variables end in `_base0` or `_base1` to indicate whether the loop starts at 0 (C-style) or 1 (Lua-style)

Examples:
```lua
---@param names string[]
---@param checker fun(name: string): boolean
---@return table<string, integer>

---@field size { w: number, h: number }

messages.reasoning_content:
The user is asking "what is the date".
I don't have a built-in tool to get the current date/time, but I can use `os.date()` in a Lua script via `run_in_neovim` to get the current system date.

However, looking at the prompt, I am an AI agent in a Neovim plugin. I should probably just answer the question if I know it or use a tool if available. I don't see a "get_date" tool, but I can execute code.

Wait, I am an LLM. I can just check my internal system clock or use a tool if I had one. Since I don't have a "get_date" tool, I'll try to run a lua command to get it from the system via the environment.

Actually, I can just use `run_process` to call `date`.

Wait, I should check if I am allowed to use `run_process`. Yes, `run_process` is in the tool list.

Let's try `date` via `run_process`.

The output of the `date` command is `Mon Jun 8 08:05:26 CDT 2026`.
I'll provide the date to the user.
